- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""M-D8 phase 1 v1 (Phase D): Parallel-fetch substrate.
2	
3	The existing live retrieval pipeline (`src/polaris_graph/
4	retrieval/live_retriever.py`) issues one fetch at a time
5	across heterogeneous backends (Serper, Semantic Scholar,
6	DuckDuckGo, etc.). M-D8 phase 1 ships the **parallel-fetch
7	substrate** that callers can wire up to those backends to
8	fan out N fetches concurrently, with per-backend
9	concurrency limits.
10	
11	## Why this milestone matters
12	
13	For predictable workloads where the URL list is known
14	ahead of time (cache warming via M-D7 phase 2, batch
15	audit re-runs, scheduled freshness checks), serial fetching
16	pays first-byte latency on every URL. Phase 1 ships the
17	substrate primitive that turns that serial loop into a
18	concurrent fan-out with operator-controllable concurrency.
19	
20	Phase 1 does NOT integrate with `live_retriever.py` itself
21	— that's phase 2 territory and requires touching production
22	retrieval flow. v1 here is the deterministic substrate the
23	integration will sit on top of.
24	
25	## What v1 ships
26	
27	  - `ParallelFetcher` Protocol — pluggable per-task fetcher
28	  - `FetchTask` dataclass — source_url + backend_id
29	  - `FetchOutcome` enum: SUCCESS | ERRORED | TIMEOUT
30	  - `FetchResultRecord` per-task outcome (renamed from
31	    FetchResult to avoid collision with M-D7 phase 2's
32	    `cache_warming.FetchResult`)
33	  - `ParallelFetchReport` aggregate
34	  - `parallel_fetch(tasks, fetcher, *, max_workers,
35	    per_backend_max_concurrent, per_task_timeout)` —
36	    pure substrate
37	
38	## Substrate boundary
39	
40	Imports stdlib only (`concurrent.futures`, `threading`,
41	`dataclasses`, `enum`). No HTTP client. No DB. No LLM. The
42	Fetcher Protocol is the seam: caller code wires up actual
43	HTTP / Crossref / Semantic Scholar clients.
44	
45	The substrate uses `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism
46	(stdlib, no async/await refactor on the caller side). Per-
47	backend concurrency limits are enforced via
48	`threading.Semaphore` instances keyed by `backend_id`.
49	
50	See `docs/md8_phase1_threat_model.md` for boundaries.
51	"""
52	
53	from __future__ import annotations
54	
55	import concurrent.futures
56	import math
57	import os
58	import threading
59	import time
60	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
61	from enum import Enum
62	from typing import Mapping, Protocol, Sequence
63	
64	
65	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
66	# Errors
67	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
68	
69	
70	class ParallelFetchError(ValueError):
71	    """Raised on contract violations — empty tasks not actually
72	    empty, bad fetcher Protocol, invalid concurrency limits."""
73	
74	
75	class FetcherProtocolError(ParallelFetchError):
76	    """Fetcher returned something other than a FetchResultRecord
77	    payload tuple. Caller-side bug — surface loudly per LAW II."""
78	
79	
80	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
81	# Task + outcome types
82	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
83	
84	
85	@dataclass(frozen=True)
86	class FetchTask:
87	    """One unit of work for the parallel fetcher.
88	
89	    `source_url` is the URL to fetch (caller's responsibility
90	    to canonicalize if needed — substrate doesn't normalize).
91	
92	    `backend_id` is the rate-limit class — Serper, Semantic
93	    Scholar, DuckDuckGo, etc. Per-backend concurrency limits
94	    use this as the semaphore key. Tasks with no specific
95	    backend should use a sentinel like `"default"`.
96	
97	    `task_metadata` is opaque caller-supplied metadata
98	    surfaced back in `FetchResultRecord.task_metadata` for
99	    correlation. Common usage: tier classification, domain
100	    tag, request-id-for-tracing. The substrate doesn't read
101	    or interpret this dict.
102	    """
103	
104	    source_url: str
105	    backend_id: str
106	    task_metadata: Mapping[str, object] = field(default_factory=dict)
107	
108	
109	class FetchOutcome(str, Enum):
110	    """Per-task outcome.
111	
112	    SUCCESS: fetcher returned a payload (regardless of HTTP
113	       status code — caller's Fetcher decides what to raise on).
114	    ERRORED: fetcher raised an exception.
115	    TIMEOUT: per-task timeout fired AFTER the task STARTED (it
116	       reached `fetcher.fetch` and over-ran its own start-anchored
117	       budget — or the global batch budget fired while it was
118	       in-flight past its deadline).
119	    NOT_DISPATCHED: the global batch budget (I-fetch-003) fired
120	       while this task was still QUEUED and had never started — the
121	       worker pool was starved by abandoned in-flight fetches, so
122	       this task never reached `fetcher.fetch`. Distinct from
123	       TIMEOUT (which means the task DID run): NOT_DISPATCHED is a
124	       never-ran non-result. Downstream consumers treat it as a
125	       non-result, the same class as TIMEOUT/ERRORED (no payload).
126	    """
127	
128	    SUCCESS = "success"
129	    ERRORED = "errored"
130	    TIMEOUT = "timeout"
131	    NOT_DISPATCHED = "not_dispatched"
132	
133	
134	@dataclass(frozen=True)
135	class FetchResultRecord:
136	    """One task's result.
137	
138	    `payload` is None unless `outcome == SUCCESS`. Type is
139	    deliberately `bytes | None` — substrate doesn't dictate
140	    whether the caller's Fetcher returns text/JSON/binary;
141	    bytes is the lowest-common-denominator transport.
142	
143	    `content_type` and `fetch_status_code` are caller-supplied
144	    metadata when SUCCESS; None otherwise.
145	
146	    `error` is None unless `outcome == ERRORED` or `TIMEOUT`,
147	    in which case it carries `str(exc)` (ERRORED) or the
148	    string `"per-task timeout exceeded"` (TIMEOUT). Exception
149	    type is NOT preserved — callers wanting structured errors
150	    should make their Fetcher catch + convert.
151	
152	    `started_at` / `finished_at` are UNIX epoch floats. For
153	    SUCCESS / ERRORED they mark when the task began (after
154	    backend semaphore acquired) and ended. For TIMEOUT,
155	    `started_at` is best-effort actual task start if the
156	    worker reached the fetch path; otherwise it falls back to
157	    the submit-time timeout-budget anchor (a task can time out
158	    before entering `fetcher.fetch` under contention).
159	
160	    `task_metadata` echoes the FetchTask's metadata for
161	    correlation.
162	    """
163	
164	    source_url: str
165	    backend_id: str
166	    outcome: FetchOutcome
167	    payload: bytes | None
168	    content_type: str | None
169	    fetch_status_code: int | None
170	    error: str | None
171	    started_at: float
172	    finished_at: float
173	    task_metadata: Mapping[str, object] = field(default_factory=dict)
174	
175	
176	@dataclass(frozen=True)
177	class ParallelFetchReport:
178	    """Aggregate of all tasks in one parallel_fetch call.
179	
180	    `started_at` / `finished_at` are wall-clock bounds of the
181	    full call (NOT sum of per-task times — they're concurrent).
182	
183	    `results` is one entry per UNIQUE (source_url, backend_id)
184	    in the input. Duplicates collapse to first-occurrence
185	    (the substrate doesn't fetch the same URL twice in one
186	    call by design).
187	
188	    `not_dispatched_count` (I-fetch-003) counts tasks the global
189	    batch budget cancelled while still QUEUED (never started) —
190	    see `FetchOutcome.NOT_DISPATCHED`. It is its own bucket so the
191	    invariant below stays honest.
192	
193	    Counts sum to `len(results)`:
194	    `success_count + errored_count + timeout_count
195	    + not_dispatched_count == len(results)`.
196	    """
197	
198	    started_at: float
199	    finished_at: float
200	    results: tuple[FetchResultRecord, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
201	    success_count: int = 0
202	    errored_count: int = 0
203	    timeout_count: int = 0
204	    not_dispatched_count: int = 0
205	
206	
207	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
208	# Fetcher Protocol
209	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
210	
211	
212	class ParallelFetcher(Protocol):
213	    """Pluggable fetcher contract — single-task, called
214	    concurrently by `parallel_fetch`.
215	
216	    Implementers MUST:
217	      - Be thread-safe (concurrent invocations from worker
218	        threads are the norm)
219	      - Return a `tuple[bytes, str, int]` of (payload,
220	        content_type, fetch_status_code) on success
221	      - Raise an exception on transport / parse failure
222	        (callers should NOT signal failure via a non-2xx
223	        status code in the returned tuple — that IS a
224	        successful fetch of an error response)
225	
226	    Implementers MUST NOT:
227	      - Mutate any cache directly (that's caller orchestration)
228	      - Block the calling thread indefinitely (use the
229	        per-task timeout if your backend doesn't enforce one)
230	    """
231	
232	    def fetch(
233	        self, task: FetchTask
234	    ) -> tuple[bytes, str, int]:
235	        ...
236	
237	
238	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
239	# Default per-backend limit
240	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
241	
242	
243	# Conservative default — any backend not explicitly configured
244	# in `per_backend_max_concurrent` gets this limit. Operators
245	# can tune for known backends (Semantic Scholar = 1 per their
246	# docs; Serper = 10; etc.) by passing a dict.
247	DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT = 4
248	
249	
250	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
251	# Harvest-loop poll interval (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C01)
252	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
253	
254	
255	# Upper bound on a single harvest-loop wait. The deadline is anchored at
256	# each task's OBSERVED start (`task_started_monotonic_by_index`), not submit,
257	# so a task that has not started yet can never be marked TIMEOUT. To detect
258	# a task that STARTS after the previous snapshot (or one that hangs) within
259	# a bounded delay of its own start-anchored budget, the harvest loop never
260	# waits unbounded: it re-snapshots start-times + recomputes the expired set
261	# at least this often. Small enough to be responsive, large enough to avoid
262	# a busy-poll. The actual wait is `min(next_deadline - now, this)`.
263	_HARVEST_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 2.0
264	
265	
266	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
267	# Global batch-budget termination guarantee (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C01 P1)
268	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
269	
270	
271	# The per-task START-anchored timeout guarantees a STARTED task terminates,
272	# but a never-started task carries a +inf deadline: if every worker thread is
273	# wedged on an abandoned (timed-out but un-cancellable) in-flight fetch, the
274	# queued siblings keep their +inf deadline and the harvest loop could spin
275	# forever. The GLOBAL batch budget is the backstop that guarantees
276	# `parallel_fetch` ALWAYS returns in bounded time even under all-workers-wedged
277	# + queued-siblings. When the budget fires, every still-remaining future is
278	# recorded — started-but-unfinished as TIMEOUT (it ran, over-ran its budget),
279	# never-started as NOT_DISPATCHED (it never reached the fetcher) — and the
280	# harvest loop breaks.
281	#
282	# Operators override the budget directly with this env var (seconds). When
283	# UNSET, the budget is DERIVED from `per_task_timeout` and the number of
284	# submission waves: `per_task_timeout * (ceil(len(deduped)/max_workers)
285	# + _BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK)`. `ceil(len/max_workers)` is the number of waves
286	# needed to run every task once if each task takes a full per_task_timeout; the
287	# slack adds head-room so a healthy batch (tasks finishing well inside budget,
288	# semaphore queuing) is never tripped spuriously.
289	PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS_ENV = (
290	    "PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS"
291	)
292	_BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK = 2
293	
294	
295	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
296	# Round-robin submit ordering (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C02)
297	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
298	
299	
300	def _round_robin_indices(
301	    tasks: Sequence[FetchTask],
302	) -> list[int]:
303	    """Return ORIGINAL task indices reordered into round-robin
304	    `backend_id` order.
305	
306	    Group indices by `backend_id` (preserving first-seen group order
307	    and intra-group input order), then emit one index per backend per
308	    round until every group is exhausted. So the first ``len(groups)``
309	    indices span distinct backends/hosts.
310	
311	    The result is a permutation of ``range(len(tasks))`` — every input
312	    index appears exactly once — so callers map results back to the
313	    ORIGINAL index unchanged. Pure / deterministic / allocation-light.
314	    """
315	    groups: dict[str, list[int]] = {}
316	    for idx, task in enumerate(tasks):
317	        groups.setdefault(task.backend_id, []).append(idx)
318	
319	    ordered: list[int] = []
320	    group_queues = list(groups.values())
321	    while group_queues:
322	        next_round: list[list[int]] = []
323	        for queue in group_queues:
324	            ordered.append(queue.pop(0))
325	            if queue:
326	                next_round.append(queue)
327	        group_queues = next_round
328	    return ordered
329	
330	
331	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
332	# Global batch-budget derivation (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C01 P1)
333	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
334	
335	
336	def _derive_batch_budget_seconds(
337	    *,
338	    num_tasks: int,
339	    max_workers: int,
340	    per_task_timeout: float | None,
341	) -> float | None:
342	    """Derive the GLOBAL batch budget (seconds) or None to disable it.
343	
344	    Precedence:
345	      1. Env `PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS` (positive float) wins.
346	      2. Else, if `per_task_timeout` is set: derive from the wave count —
347	         `per_task_timeout * (ceil(num_tasks / max_workers)
348	         + _BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK)`. The ceil term is how many sequential
349	         waves it takes to run every task once if each consumes a full
350	         per_task_timeout; the slack is head-room so a healthy batch is never
351	         tripped spuriously.
352	      3. Else (no env AND no per-task timeout): None — preserve the existing
353	         "None disables timeout" contract. With no per-task budget there is no
354	         natural time-scale to derive from; the caller opted out of bounding.
355	
356	    An env value that is empty, non-numeric, or <= 0 is ignored (falls through
357	    to the derived/None path) — a malformed knob must never silently disable
358	    the termination guarantee when a per_task_timeout is in force.
359	    """
360	    env_raw = os.environ.get(PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS_ENV)
361	    if env_raw is not None and env_raw.strip():
362	        try:
363	            env_val = float(env_raw)
364	        except ValueError:
365	            env_val = None
366	        if env_val is not None and env_val > 0:
367	            return env_val
368	
369	    if per_task_timeout is None:
370	        return None
371	
372	    waves = math.ceil(num_tasks / max_workers) + _BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK
373	    return per_task_timeout * waves
374	
375	
376	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
377	# Public API
378	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
379	
380	
381	def parallel_fetch(
382	    tasks: Sequence[FetchTask],
383	    fetcher: ParallelFetcher,
384	    *,
385	    max_workers: int = 8,
386	    per_backend_max_concurrent: Mapping[str, int] | None = None,
387	    per_task_timeout: float | None = None,
388	    overall_deadline_monotonic: float | None = None,
389	) -> ParallelFetchReport:
390	    """Fan out `tasks` to a thread pool and collect results.
391	
392	    `max_workers` caps the global concurrency. Per-backend
393	    limits gate concurrent-by-backend further: a task on
394	    backend `"semantic_scholar"` can only proceed when both a
395	    worker is free AND the backend's semaphore has capacity.
396	
397	    `per_backend_max_concurrent` maps `backend_id` to its
398	    concurrency limit. Backends absent from the map use
399	    `DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT`. Pass `{"semantic_scholar": 1,
400	    "serper": 10, ...}` to set explicit ceilings.
401	
402	    `per_task_timeout` is the wall-clock seconds before a
403	    task is marked `TIMEOUT`. None disables timeout.
404	    The cancel attempt is best-effort: in-flight tasks may
405	    continue running after the timeout fires (Python's
406	    Future.cancel() can't interrupt synchronous code), but
407	    the report records TIMEOUT and the worker thread is
408	    abandoned. Backend semaphore is released either way (in a
409	    finally block), so subsequent tasks on the same backend
410	    don't deadlock waiting on a leaked permit.
411	
412	    `overall_deadline_monotonic` (I-deepfix-001 P1-3 #1344) is an
413	    ABSOLUTE `time.monotonic()` instant that CAPS the global batch
414	    deadline: the effective batch deadline becomes
415	    `min(own_batch_budget_deadline, overall_deadline_monotonic)`.
416	    None (default) preserves the existing behavior (the batch budget
417	    is derived from `per_task_timeout`/the env knob only). This lets a
418	    caller's retrieval-phase wall actually bound the fetch grind: the
419	    derived batch budget (per_task_timeout * waves) can be many times
420	    the wall, so without this cap the wall could not stop the fetch.
421	    A value already in the PAST is honored — the batch returns on the
422	    next poll with the still-remaining futures recorded as TIMEOUT /
423	    NOT_DISPATCHED (a bounded immediate return, NOT an error).
424	
425	    Returns a `ParallelFetchReport` summarizing per-task
426	    outcomes. Order of `results` is the input task order,
427	    NOT completion order — callers correlate via task_metadata
428	    if they need other orderings.
429	
430	    Duplicate (source_url, backend_id) pairs in input collapse
431	    to first-occurrence (subsequent dupes dropped silently).
432	    """
433	    if not isinstance(tasks, Sequence) or isinstance(
434	        tasks, (str, bytes)
435	    ):
436	        raise ParallelFetchError(
437	            f"tasks must be a sequence of FetchTask, got "
438	            f"{type(tasks).__name__}"
439	        )
440	    if fetcher is None or not hasattr(fetcher, "fetch"):
441	        raise ParallelFetchError(
442	            "fetcher must implement the ParallelFetcher Protocol"
443	        )
444	    if max_workers < 1:
445	        raise ParallelFetchError(
446	            f"max_workers must be >= 1, got {max_workers}"
447	        )
448	    if per_task_timeout is not None and per_task_timeout <= 0:
449	        raise ParallelFetchError(
450	            f"per_task_timeout must be > 0 or None, got "
451	            f"{per_task_timeout}"
452	        )
453	
454	    if per_backend_max_concurrent is not None:
455	        for k, v in per_backend_max_concurrent.items():
456	            if not isinstance(v, int) or v < 1:
457	                raise ParallelFetchError(
458	                    f"per_backend_max_concurrent[{k!r}] must be "
459	                    f"a positive int, got {v!r}"
460	                )
461	
462	    # Build deduplicated task list (first wins).
463	    seen_keys: set[tuple[str, str]] = set()
464	    deduped: list[FetchTask] = []
465	    for i, task in enumerate(tasks):
466	        if not isinstance(task, FetchTask):
467	            raise ParallelFetchError(
468	                f"tasks[{i}] must be FetchTask, got "
469	                f"{type(task).__name__}"
470	            )
471	        key = (task.source_url, task.backend_id)
472	        if key in seen_keys:
473	            continue
474	        seen_keys.add(key)
475	        deduped.append(task)
476	
477	    started_at = time.time()
478	
479	    if not deduped:
480	        return ParallelFetchReport(
481	            started_at=started_at,
482	            finished_at=started_at,
483	            results=(),
484	            success_count=0,
485	            errored_count=0,
486	            timeout_count=0,
487	        )
488	
489	    # Per-backend semaphores. Lazily allocated on first task
490	    # for each backend.
491	    backend_limits: dict[str, int] = dict(per_backend_max_concurrent or {})
492	    semaphores: dict[str, threading.Semaphore] = {}
493	    semaphores_lock = threading.Lock()
494	
495	    def _get_semaphore(backend_id: str) -> threading.Semaphore:
496	        with semaphores_lock:
497	            sem = semaphores.get(backend_id)
498	            if sem is None:
499	                limit = backend_limits.get(
500	                    backend_id, DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT,
501	                )
502	                sem = threading.Semaphore(limit)
503	                semaphores[backend_id] = sem
504	            return sem
505	
506	    # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01): the deadline is START-anchored, so we record the
507	    # MONOTONIC start of each task (immune to wall-clock jumps) keyed by index
508	    # under the lock. The wall-clock start is kept separately ONLY for the
509	    # FetchResultRecord.started_at report field (AC7 — never used in deadline
510	    # arithmetic). A not-yet-started index is absent from BOTH maps → +inf
511	    # effective deadline → cannot TIMEOUT before it runs.
512	    task_started_monotonic_by_index: dict[int, float] = {}
513	    task_started_wall_by_index: dict[int, float] = {}
514	    task_started_lock = threading.Lock()
515	
516	    def _run_task(
517	        index: int, task: FetchTask,
518	    ) -> FetchResultRecord:
519	        sem = _get_semaphore(task.backend_id)
520	        sem.acquire()
521	        task_started = time.time()
522	        task_started_mono = time.monotonic()
523	        with task_started_lock:
524	            task_started_monotonic_by_index[index] = task_started_mono
525	            task_started_wall_by_index[index] = task_started
526	        try:
527	            try:
528	                result = fetcher.fetch(task)
529	            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — intentional broad
530	                return FetchResultRecord(
531	                    source_url=task.source_url,
532	                    backend_id=task.backend_id,
533	                    outcome=FetchOutcome.ERRORED,
534	                    payload=None,
535	                    content_type=None,
536	                    fetch_status_code=None,
537	                    error=str(exc),
538	                    started_at=task_started,
539	                    finished_at=time.time(),
540	                    task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
541	                )
542	
543	            if (
544	                not isinstance(result, tuple)
545	                or len(result) != 3
546	                or not isinstance(result[0], (bytes, bytearray))
547	                or not isinstance(result[1], str)
548	                or not isinstance(result[2], int)
549	            ):
550	                raise FetcherProtocolError(
551	                    f"fetcher.fetch({task.source_url!r}) returned "
552	                    f"{type(result).__name__}, expected "
553	                    "tuple[bytes, str, int]"
554	                )
555	
556	            payload, content_type, status = result
557	            return FetchResultRecord(
558	                source_url=task.source_url,
559	                backend_id=task.backend_id,
560	                outcome=FetchOutcome.SUCCESS,
561	                payload=bytes(payload),
562	                content_type=content_type,
563	                fetch_status_code=status,
564	                error=None,
565	                started_at=task_started,
566	                finished_at=time.time(),
567	                task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
568	            )
569	        finally:
570	            sem.release()
571	
572	    results_by_index: dict[int, FetchResultRecord] = {}
573	
574	    # Codex round-1 HIGH fix (v2): manage executor manually so we
575	    # can shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) on TIMEOUT.
576	    # The `with` block's __exit__ calls shutdown(wait=True) which
577	    # blocks until all in-flight workers finish — defeating
578	    # boundary 3's caller-latency promise.
579	    executor = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(
580	        max_workers=max_workers,
581	    )
582	    try:
583	        future_to_index: dict[concurrent.futures.Future, int] = {}
584	        # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C02): submit in ROUND-ROBIN backend order so the
585	        # first `max_workers` submissions span up to `max_workers` distinct
586	        # backends/hosts. Without this, a clustered same-host candidate prefix
587	        # makes the first workers all block on ONE host-semaphore (acquired
588	        # inside `_run_task`), starving distinct-host tasks queued behind them.
589	        # `_round_robin_indices` returns ORIGINAL deduped indices, so result
590	        # indexing into `results_by_index` is unchanged (correctness preserved).
591	        submit_order = _round_robin_indices(deduped)
592	        # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01 P1): anchor the GLOBAL batch budget at submit.
593	        batch_start = time.monotonic()
594	        for idx in submit_order:
595	            task = deduped[idx]
596	            fut = executor.submit(_run_task, idx, task)
597	            future_to_index[fut] = idx
598	
599	        # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01 P1): derive the GLOBAL batch budget — the
600	        # termination backstop that guarantees this call returns in bounded
601	        # time even when every worker is wedged on an abandoned in-flight fetch
602	        # and the queued siblings keep their +inf per-task deadline. Env wins;
603	        # else derive from per_task_timeout and the wave count; else (no
604	        # per-task timeout AND no env) the batch budget is disabled (None),
605	        # preserving the existing "None disables timeout" contract.
606	        batch_budget = _derive_batch_budget_seconds(
607	            num_tasks=len(deduped),
608	            max_workers=max_workers,
609	            per_task_timeout=per_task_timeout,
610	        )
611	
612	        remaining: set[concurrent.futures.Future] = set(future_to_index)
613	        protocol_error_to_raise: FetcherProtocolError | None = None
614	
615	        def _effective_deadline_monotonic(idx: int) -> float:
616	            # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01): START-anchored, MONOTONIC effective
617	            # deadline. A not-yet-started index (absent from the start map)
618	            # → +inf: it CANNOT TIMEOUT before it runs. A started index →
619	            # its OWN observed start + per_task_timeout. Caller holds
620	            # `task_started_lock`.
621	            started = task_started_monotonic_by_index.get(idx)
622	            if started is None:
623	                return float("inf")
624	            return started + per_task_timeout  # type: ignore[operator]
625	
626	        batch_deadline = (
627	            batch_start + batch_budget if batch_budget is not None else None
628	        )
629	        # I-deepfix-001 P1-3 (#1344): cap the batch deadline by the caller's overall
630	        # retrieval-phase wall (an absolute time.monotonic() instant, same domain as
631	        # batch_start). Effective deadline = min(own budget deadline, overall wall) so
632	        # the retrieval wall can actually stop the fetch grind (the derived budget,
633	        # per_task_timeout * waves, can be many times the wall). When the own budget is
634	        # disabled (None) but a wall is supplied, the wall alone bounds the batch. A
635	        # past wall is honored: the harvest loop returns on the next bounded poll with
636	        # the remaining futures recorded TIMEOUT / NOT_DISPATCHED (bounded immediate
637	        # return, never an error).
638	        if overall_deadline_monotonic is not None:
639	            batch_deadline = (
640	                overall_deadline_monotonic
641	                if batch_deadline is None
642	                else min(batch_deadline, overall_deadline_monotonic)
643	            )
644	
645	        while remaining and protocol_error_to_raise is None:
646	            if per_task_timeout is None and batch_deadline is None:
647	                # No per-task timeout AND no batch budget — just wait for the
648	                # next completion (the original "None disables timeout" path).
649	                done, _ = concurrent.futures.wait(
650	                    remaining,
651	                    return_when=concurrent.futures.FIRST_COMPLETED,
652	                )
653	            else:
654	                # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01): BOUNDED-POLL harvest. The wait is
655	                # ALWAYS bounded by `_HARVEST_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS`, never
656	                # `timeout=None`, so a task that STARTS after this snapshot
657	                # (or one that hangs), AND the GLOBAL batch deadline, are
658	                # re-checked within the poll interval — the harvest never
659	                # relies on a sibling completing to make progress. When there
660	                # is no per-task timeout (batch budget only), the per-task
661	                # deadlines are all +inf so the poll interval alone bounds the
662	                # wait until the batch deadline fires.
663	                now_mono = time.monotonic()
664	                if per_task_timeout is None:
665	                    next_deadline = float("inf")
666	                else:
667	                    with task_started_lock:
668	                        next_deadline = min(
669	                            _effective_deadline_monotonic(future_to_index[f])
670	                            for f in remaining
671	                        )
672	                if batch_deadline is not None:
673	                    next_deadline = min(next_deadline, batch_deadline)
674	                wait_timeout = min(
675	                    max(0.0, next_deadline - now_mono),
676	                    _HARVEST_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS,
677	                )
678	                done, _ = concurrent.futures.wait(
679	                    remaining,
680	                    timeout=wait_timeout,
681	                    return_when=concurrent.futures.FIRST_COMPLETED,
682	                )
683	
684	            if done:
685	                for fut in done:
686	                    idx = future_to_index[fut]
687	                    task = deduped[idx]
688	                    try:
689	                        rec = fut.result()
690	                    except FetcherProtocolError as exc:
691	                        protocol_error_to_raise = exc
692	                        break
693	                    results_by_index[idx] = rec
694	                    remaining.discard(fut)
695	
696	            if protocol_error_to_raise is not None:
697	                break
698	
699	            if per_task_timeout is not None:
700	                # Recompute the expired set from CURRENT start-times after
701	                # every wake (whether or not anything completed). A task is
702	                # TIMEOUT iff IT exceeded ITS OWN start-anchored budget.
703	                now_mono = time.monotonic()
704	                with task_started_lock:
705	                    expired = [
706	                        f for f in list(remaining)
707	                        if _effective_deadline_monotonic(
708	                            future_to_index[f]
709	                        ) <= now_mono
710	                    ]
711	                if expired:
712	                    now_wall = time.time()
713	                    for fut in expired:
714	                        idx = future_to_index[fut]
715	                        task = deduped[idx]
716	                        fut.cancel()  # best-effort
717	                        with task_started_lock:
718	                            timeout_started_at = (
719	                                task_started_wall_by_index.get(idx, now_wall)
720	                            )
721	                        results_by_index[idx] = FetchResultRecord(
722	                            source_url=task.source_url,
723	                            backend_id=task.backend_id,
724	                            outcome=FetchOutcome.TIMEOUT,
725	                            payload=None,
726	                            content_type=None,
727	                            fetch_status_code=None,
728	                            error="per-task timeout exceeded",
729	                            started_at=timeout_started_at,
730	                            finished_at=now_wall,
731	                            task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
732	                        )
733	                        remaining.discard(fut)
734	
735	            # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01 P1): GLOBAL batch-budget termination
736	            # guarantee. If the batch deadline has passed, the batch did NOT
737	            # converge in bounded time — almost always because every worker is
738	            # wedged on an abandoned (timed-out but un-cancellable) in-flight
739	            # fetch while distinct-host siblings sit queued behind it forever.
740	            # Record EVERY still-remaining future (not just never-started ones,
741	            # or the final `results_by_index[i]` reduction KeyErrors on a
742	            # started-but-unfinished index) and break so the call ALWAYS
743	            # returns:
744	            #   - started-but-unfinished (idx in the start map) → TIMEOUT (it
745	            #     ran and over-ran; same class as a normal per-task timeout).
746	            #   - never-started (idx absent from the start map) → NOT_DISPATCHED
747	            #     (it never reached the fetcher; distinct outcome, NOT a
748	            #     mislabelled TIMEOUT).
749	            if (
750	                batch_deadline is not None
751	                and remaining
752	                and time.monotonic() > batch_deadline
753	            ):
754	                now_wall = time.time()
755	                for fut in list(remaining):
756	                    idx = future_to_index[fut]
757	                    task = deduped[idx]
758	                    fut.cancel()  # best-effort
759	                    with task_started_lock:
760	                        was_started = (
761	                            idx in task_started_monotonic_by_index
762	                        )
763	                        started_wall = task_started_wall_by_index.get(
764	                            idx, now_wall,
765	                        )
766	                    if was_started:
767	                        results_by_index[idx] = FetchResultRecord(
768	                            source_url=task.source_url,
769	                            backend_id=task.backend_id,
770	                            outcome=FetchOutcome.TIMEOUT,
771	                            payload=None,
772	                            content_type=None,
773	                            fetch_status_code=None,
774	                            error="per-task timeout exceeded",
775	                            started_at=started_wall,
776	                            finished_at=now_wall,
777	                            task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
778	                        )
779	                    else:
780	                        results_by_index[idx] = FetchResultRecord(
781	                            source_url=task.source_url,
782	                            backend_id=task.backend_id,
783	                            outcome=FetchOutcome.NOT_DISPATCHED,
784	                            payload=None,
785	                            content_type=None,
786	                            fetch_status_code=None,
787	                            error=(
788	                                "never dispatched: parallel-fetch batch "
789	                                "budget exceeded (worker pool starved by "
790	                                "abandoned in-flight tasks)"
791	                            ),
792	                            started_at=now_wall,
793	                            finished_at=now_wall,
794	                            task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
795	                        )
796	                    remaining.discard(fut)
797	                break
798	
799	        if protocol_error_to_raise is not None:
800	            for other_fut in remaining:
801	                other_fut.cancel()
802	            # Codex round-1 MEDIUM fix (v2): release the executor
803	            # without waiting so the protocol error propagates
804	            # promptly. In-flight workers continue but caller
805	            # gets control back.
806	            executor.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)
807	            raise protocol_error_to_raise
808	    finally:
809	        # Codex round-1 HIGH fix (v2): non-blocking shutdown.
810	        # Pending (not-yet-started) futures are cancelled;
811	        # in-flight workers continue but don't gate our return.
812	        executor.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)
813	
814	    # Reorder by input index.
815	    results = tuple(
816	        results_by_index[i] for i in range(len(deduped))
817	    )
818	
819	    success = sum(1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.SUCCESS)
820	    errored = sum(1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.ERRORED)
821	    timed_out = sum(1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.TIMEOUT)
822	    not_dispatched = sum(
823	        1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.NOT_DISPATCHED
824	    )
825	
826	    return ParallelFetchReport(
827	        started_at=started_at,
828	        finished_at=time.time(),
829	        results=results,
830	        success_count=success,
831	        errored_count=errored,
832	        timeout_count=timed_out,
833	        not_dispatched_count=not_dispatched,
834	    )
835	
836	
837	def report_to_exit_code(report: ParallelFetchReport) -> int:
838	    """Map outcome to CI exit code.
839	
840	    Convention: any errored OR timeout OR not-dispatched → 1;
841	    all-success or empty → 0. NOT_DISPATCHED (I-fetch-003) is a
842	    starvation failure — a batch that never ran its queued tasks
843	    must NOT report success, so it is treated the same as TIMEOUT.
844	    """
845	    if (
846	        report.errored_count > 0
847	        or report.timeout_count > 0
848	        or report.not_dispatched_count > 0
849	    ):
850	        return 1
851	    return 0
852

1	"""Parser runner abstraction (M-11 — Phase B).
2	
3	Per FINAL_PLAN.md: each upload type needs an extractor that
4	preserves location-aware provenance. Phase B ships:
5	  - `ParserRunner` ABC: standard interface for any file type.
6	  - `TextParser`: real implementation for plain-text uploads
7	    (.txt / .md). Emits TextSpan provenance.
8	  - `PdfParser`: stub that raises NotImplementedError until Phase
9	    C wires PyMuPDF / pdfplumber. Reserved as a placeholder so the
10	    state machine + API can be exercised end-to-end without
11	    blocking on the heavy dep.
12	
13	The abstraction is the deliverable; only TextParser is wired into
14	the inspector_router for Phase B.
15	"""
16	
17	from __future__ import annotations
18	
19	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
20	from dataclasses import dataclass
21	from pathlib import Path
22	from typing import Any
23	
24	from src.polaris_graph.audit_ir.provenance import (
25	    PdfSpan,
26	    TextSpan,
27	    UploadProvenance,
28	    to_dict,
29	)
30	
31	
32	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
33	# Result type
34	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
35	
36	
37	@dataclass(frozen=True)
38	class ParseResult:
39	    """Output of `ParserRunner.parse`. Each chunk pairs a slice of
40	    extracted text with its provenance."""
41	
42	    chunks: tuple[tuple[str, UploadProvenance], ...]
43	
44	
45	def parse_result_to_chunk_dicts(
46	    result: ParseResult,
47	) -> list[tuple[str, dict[str, Any]]]:
48	    """Convert ParseResult to the (text, provenance_dict) tuples
49	    that WorkspaceStore.insert_chunks expects."""
50	    return [(text, to_dict(prov)) for text, prov in result.chunks]
51	
52	
53	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
54	# Errors
55	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
56	
57	
58	class ParserError(Exception):
59	    """Raised when parsing fails. Caller catches this and
60	    transitions the upload to status='failed'."""
61	
62	
63	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
64	# Abstract base
65	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
66	
67	
68	class ParserRunner(ABC):
69	    """One parser per file family. Stateless — instances can be
70	    shared across requests."""
71	
72	    parser_id: str  # subclass must set
73	
74	    @abstractmethod
75	    def can_handle(self, filename: str, content_type: str | None) -> bool:
76	        """Return True if this parser handles the given upload."""
77	
78	    @abstractmethod
79	    def parse(self, upload_id: str, storage_path: Path) -> ParseResult:
80	        """Parse the file at `storage_path` into provenance-tagged
81	        chunks. Raises ParserError on any failure."""
82	
83	
84	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
85	# TextParser — Phase B real implementation
86	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
87	
88	
89	# Default chunking parameters. Env-overridable per LAW VI.
90	DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_CHARS = 1500
91	
92	
93	class TextParser(ParserRunner):
94	    """Plain-text parser for .txt / .md uploads.
95	
96	    Splits the file into fixed-size character chunks. Each chunk
97	    carries a TextSpan provenance with char offsets relative to the
98	    full upload text. Chunk size is env-configurable via
99	    `PG_TEXT_CHUNK_CHARS` (per LAW VI).
100	    """
101	
102	    parser_id = "text"
103	
104	    # Codex M-11 review fix: narrow to plain-text only. Originally
105	    # any `text/*` MIME claimed by TextParser, which silently
106	    # absorbed `text/csv` / `text/tab-separated-values` and stored
107	    # spreadsheet-shaped uploads as TextSpan provenance. M-12 would
108	    # then need re-upload to get SheetCell. The Phase B safe rule:
109	    # only accept extensions and MIMEs that are unambiguously plain
110	    # text. Sheet-like formats fall through to "no parser" → status
111	    # `pending` until Phase C ships a SheetParser.
112	    _SUPPORTED_EXT: frozenset[str] = frozenset({".txt", ".md", ".text"})
113	    _SUPPORTED_MIMES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({
114	        "text/plain", "text/markdown", "text/x-markdown",
115	    })
116	
117	    def __init__(self, chunk_chars: int | None = None) -> None:
118	        self._chunk_chars = chunk_chars or self._env_chunk_chars()
119	
120	    @staticmethod
121	    def _env_chunk_chars() -> int:
122	        import os
123	        raw = os.environ.get("PG_TEXT_CHUNK_CHARS")
124	        if raw is None:
125	            return DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_CHARS
126	        try:
127	            return max(100, int(raw))
128	        except ValueError:
129	            return DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_CHARS
130	
131	    def can_handle(self, filename: str, content_type: str | None) -> bool:
132	        # Codex M-11 v2 review fix: strip MIME parameters before
133	        # comparing. Real upload headers often include "; charset=
134	        # utf-8" / "; charset=ascii" suffixes; the v1 exact-match
135	        # rejected those legitimate plain-text uploads.
136	        if content_type:
137	            base_mime = content_type.split(";", 1)[0].strip().lower()
138	            if base_mime in self._SUPPORTED_MIMES:
139	                return True
140	        ext = Path(filename).suffix.lower()
141	        return ext in self._SUPPORTED_EXT
142	
143	    def parse(self, upload_id: str, storage_path: Path) -> ParseResult:
144	        try:
145	            text = storage_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
146	        except FileNotFoundError as exc:
147	            raise ParserError(f"upload file missing: {storage_path}") from exc
148	        except OSError as exc:
149	            raise ParserError(f"could not read upload: {exc}") from exc
150	
151	        if not text:
152	            return ParseResult(chunks=())
153	
154	        chunks: list[tuple[str, UploadProvenance]] = []
155	        n = self._chunk_chars
156	        for start in range(0, len(text), n):
157	            end = min(start + n, len(text))
158	            chunk_text = text[start:end]
159	            prov = TextSpan(
160	                upload_id=upload_id, char_start=start, char_end=end,
161	            )
162	            chunks.append((chunk_text, prov))
163	        return ParseResult(chunks=tuple(chunks))
164	
165	
166	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
167	# PdfParser — Phase B stub
168	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
169	
170	
171	class PdfParser(ParserRunner):
172	    """Phase B stub. Phase C will wire a real extractor (PyMuPDF /
173	    pdfplumber) and emit PdfSpan provenance with page-relative
174	    char offsets.
175	
176	    Marked as not-yet-implemented so the upload pipeline can route
177	    PDFs to operator review rather than silently mis-parsing them.
178	    """
179	
180	    parser_id = "pdf"
181	
182	    def can_handle(self, filename: str, content_type: str | None) -> bool:
183	        if content_type == "application/pdf":
184	            return True
185	        return Path(filename).suffix.lower() == ".pdf"
186	
187	    def parse(self, upload_id: str, storage_path: Path) -> ParseResult:
188	        # Reserve the PdfSpan symbol so the schema is reachable even
189	        # while the parser is stubbed (linters won't drop the import).
190	        _ = PdfSpan
191	        raise ParserError(
192	            "PDF parsing not yet supported in Phase B; the schema reserves "
193	            "PdfSpan provenance for Phase C M-11.5 (PyMuPDF/pdfplumber)."
194	        )
195	
196	
197	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
198	# Registry
199	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
200	
201	
202	_DEFAULT_PARSERS: tuple[ParserRunner, ...] = (TextParser(), PdfParser())
203	
204	
205	def select_parser(
206	    filename: str,
207	    content_type: str | None,
208	    parsers: tuple[ParserRunner, ...] = _DEFAULT_PARSERS,
209	) -> ParserRunner | None:
210	    """Pick the first parser that claims it can handle this upload,
211	    or None if no registered parser matches."""
212	    for p in parsers:
213	        if p.can_handle(filename, content_type):
214	            return p
215	    return None
216
